coredata.dc:title: Concentration modulated photoluminescence and optical switching performance of graphene-oxide quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Kun
Ma, Yong-Fu
Zhang, Ya-Qi
Gao, Han
Han, Yi-Bo
coredata.dc:description: Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) have drawn much attention for their modulated bandgap rendering their application in optoelectronic devices. In this study, we present comprehensive photoluminescence properties of GOQDs and its related optical transitions, which agree with recent theoretical predications. In particular, ormosil gel glasses doped GOQDs was synthesized to achieve tunable photoluminescence. We also report the third-order nonlinear optical properties of the quantum dots, which were studied by using Z-scan technique. The evaluated one-photon and two-photon figures of merit demonstrate that GOQDs could be a promising candidate for the optical switching applications in a broad wavelength region.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Quantum dot
Photoluminescence
Optical nonlinearity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectrum of the GOQDs in aqueous dispersion. Inset: Bandgap evaluation from the Tauc-plot equation.

(a) Excitation-emission PL spectra for GOQDs. (b) PLE spectra with detection wavelength of 513 nm and 540 nm. (c) a schematic illustration of energy level distribution and electronic transition processes for GOQDs. (d) Fluorescence decay for GOQDs peaking at 514 nm and 540 nm, solid lines are exponential decay fit.

(a) Absorption spectra and (b) PL spectra of GOQDs in ormosil gel glasses with different concentrations, inset of (b) shows the picture of the gel-samples. (c) Fluorescence decay curves for corresponding GOQDs, solid lines are exponential decay fit. Inset show the lifetime at different concentrations.

(a) Closed-aperture and (b) open-aperture Z-scan curves at different wavelengths. Open circles are experimental data and solid lines are fit curved according to Eq. (1) and Eq. (2), respectively. (c) Optical NLA coefficient β and NLR index γ versus laser wavelength at incident power of 50 mW. (d) The logarithmic peak-valley relative transmittance versus laser power at 720 nm, solid line is the linear fit.
Wavelength dependent W and T values for GOQDs.
Nonlinear absorption coefficient β, nonlinear refraction index γ at specific wavalength of GOQDs in the current study and other materials from previous reports.

head.ce:title: Concentration modulated photoluminescence and optical switching performance of graphene-oxide quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wuhan National High Magnetic Field Center and School of Physics, Huazhong University of Science & Technology, Luoyu road 1037, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Wuhan National High Magnetic Field Center and School of Physics, Huazhong University of Science & Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) have drawn much attention for their modulated bandgap rendering their application in optoelectronic devices. In this study, we present comprehensive photoluminescence properties of GOQDs and its related optical transitions, which agree with recent theoretical predications. In particular, ormosil gel glasses doped GOQDs was synthesized to achieve tunable photoluminescence. We also report the third-order nonlinear optical properties of the quantum dots, which were studied by using Z-scan technique. The evaluated one-photon and two-photon figures of merit demonstrate that GOQDs could be a promising candidate for the optical switching applications in a broad wavelength region.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, since it was first discovered [1], has received considerable attention due to its unique electronic and mechanical properties, and could be exploited in a wide range of applications [2–4]. However, as graphene is a zero-gap semimetal, its electronic band structure could not be manipulated, which impose restrictions on most optoelectronic applications. Graphene oxide (GO), as a derivative of graphene, is modified by oxygen groups on the basal plane and various other types at the edges, and make up for graphene's shortage [5,6]. With various oxygen groups, graphene oxide may further alter the optical properties or even add new functionalities [7–10]. Nano-sized graphene oxide materials, including graphene oxide nanosheets, graphene oxide nanoribbons, graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) [11–14], and so on, possess not only great optical nonlinearity, but also bright visible-near infrared luminescence [15,16]. Besides, tunable fluorescence emission has been obtained and recognized as a key requirement for its potential use in low cost, large area optoelectronic devices [17–19].

Up to now, many studies on the photoluminescence (PL) properties of GOQDs have reported. The characteristics of PL originates from the sp 2 domains in the sp 3 matrix, and quantum confinement effect is often considered. Through varying the particles size or functional group on the surface of GOQDs [20–23], the PL can be tuned from blue to red. Further, the excitation wavelength and acid-based environment may also affect the intensity and wavelength of PL [24–26]. In addition to the PL properties, the GOQDs also show promising nonlinear optical properties [27]. E. Sakho et al. revealed a predominantly absorption saturation behavior of GOQDs employing nanosecond laser pulses at 532 nm [28]; Y. Gao et al. presented the nonlinear optical and optical limiting of GOQDs compared with the graphene oxide nanosheets and nanoribbons [29]; and Z. Liu et al. investigated the nonlinear optical properties of GO in nanosecond and picosecond regimes [30]. However, limited experimental results were reported on the electronic structure, optical transitions, and the related wavelength dependent optical nonlinear susceptibilities [31].

In this study, the linear and nonlinear optical properties of GOQDs are comprehensively investigated. The absorption and excitation-emission PL spectrum explains the bright electronic energy levels for GOQDs; a concentration dependent emission is presented for GOQDs dispersed in ormosil gel glasses; and the third-order optical nonlinearity of GOQDs was investigated by using Z-scan technique in a wide wavelength range.

Graphene oxide quantum dots powder and aqueous solution (thickness: 1–2 nm, size: <10 nm) were obtained from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). The corresponding nanomaterial characterization, including transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, were described in previous study [32,33]. The identified functional groups, including hydroxyl, epoxy, carboxyl and carbonyl, were connected with the edge and surface of the host. The GOQDs dispersed in ormosil gel glasses were synthesis by a typical sol-gel method. The silicate tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), absolute ethanol and deionized water acted as reactants and the molar ratio of them were 1: 4: 4. The different dispersion concentrations of GOQDs were 100 mg, 50 mg, 25 mg, 10 mg and 2 mg to 1 mol TEOS. These raw materials were mixed evenly by ultrasonic treatment. During this process, HCl was added dropwise until the PH value was reduced to approximately 2. Finally, this solution was aged for about one hours at 30 °C until the complete hydrolysis of the silicate completes, and casted into polystyrene petri dish to form GOQDs-Ormosil hybrid gel glasses.

The absorption spectra were recorded by a US-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 950) and the excitation-emission PL was measured by a Fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax Plus, Horiba). A mode-locked Ti: Sapphire laser (Mira900, Coherent) with repetition rate of 76 MHz and pulse duration of 130 fs was used as the laser source for PL-time decay and Z-scan measurements. A time-resolved single-photon counting system (PicoHarp300, Picoquant) was used to realize the time-resolved PL measurements.

The UV–VIS–NIR absorption spectrum of the aqueous solution was shown in Fig. 1. A broad absorption band was observed in the range from 200 to 600 nm. The maximal absorbance is located at 230 nm due to a π→π* transition of the C˭C bond, which dominate the GOQDs structures. The absorption edge can be attributed to the n→π* transition of the C˭O or C‒O bonds [9]. As we know, graphene-oxide materials behave like direct-gap semiconductors because the quantum-sized sp 2 domains could localize electron-hole pairs and create energy bandgaps [27]. The optical band gap (E g) could be determined by using the Tauc equation: α0hν = A(hν - E g)1/2, where α0 is the linear absorption coefficient, h is the Planck's constant, ν is the incident light frequency and A is a constant. By plotting (α0hν)2 against hν, the optical band gap was determined by extrapolating the linear segment on the Tauc plot to the photon energy axis. As shown in inset of Fig. 1, the value of E g is about 3.6 eV.

The full excitation-emission spectra for GOQDs are shown in Fig. 2(a). It is clear that two emission bands locate at 513 nm and 540 nm do not shift with excitation wavelength but exhibit different intensities. The strongest emission is observed at the excitation wavelength of 250 nm and 450 nm. The emission band at 513 nm is more sensitive to the excitation of 250 nm, while the 540 nm emission is stronger at the excitation of 460 nm. This result agrees well with the theoretical predictions reported by D. Bhatnagar [34], where the energy bands and density of states (DOS) were calculated by using density functional theory (DFT). Two transition paths were given as shown in Fig. 2(c): an electron on the valence band (VB) at around −2.7 eV was excited to the conduction band (CB) at 2.2 eV, and the other on the VB at −1.4 eV to the CB at 1.5 eV. Finally, the electron relaxes to lower energy levels of CB through non-radiative process, and then return the VB to give rise to the PL. So, the excitation at 250 nm is related to the first transition path, while the excitation at 450 to the second path. The PL decay at the two peaks position were measured. It is note that the lifetime for the first emission band is 1.6 ns, much larger than 0.6 ns for the second. The two PL bands is attributed to the electron-hole recombination from the bottom of CB, and neighbor localized sates, to the valence band, respectively. The longer lifetime at 510 nm corresponds to the band edge recombination, while the shorter one to the localized electronic states recombination [35–37].

It is found that the GOQDs is easily to be dispersed into ormosil gel glasses, and form a crystal-like phosphor with stable and concentration modulated PL properties. As shown in inset of Fig. 3(a), the samples with different concentration were labeled as C, 5 C, 12.5 C, 25 C and 50 C, corresponding to 2 mg, 10 mg, 25 mg, 50 mg and 100 mg of GOQDs dispersed in 1 mol TEOS, respectively. The transmittance of the hybrid glasses gradually decreases with increasing GOQDs concentrations. Fig. 3(a) presents optical absorption spectra for the glass with different concentration. It is apparent that the absorption becomes larger at increasing concentration of the GOQDs. The PL spectra of the gel glasses show broad emission band with peaks at around 517 nm (C), 540 nm (5 C), 586 nm (12.5 C), 611 nm (25 C), and 627 nm (50 C) at the excitation wavelength of 430 nm, as shown in Fig. 3(b).

The PL and optical absorption spectra could be understood by self-absorption [38]. At low concentration from C to 5 C, the PL intensity increases due to increasing QDs concentration. At this situation, the concentration is not high enough to cause remarkable self-absorption, so the redshift is negligible. When the concentration further increases, the absorption in the visible wavelength range becomes larger, and the PL intensity drops dramatically. Since the absorption in the short wavelength region is stronger than that of the long wavelength region, the PL at the blue side is reabsorbed by the sample, which modulates the shape of the spectra and causes the visualized redshift. To get further insight into the mechanism of this effect, the PL decay was measured at the peak position for each sample. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the PL lifetime of GOQDs decrease from C to 5 C, which is due to the localized electronic states recombination as described above. While it becomes longer at increasing concentration which agrees with the mechanism of self-absorption [39,40].

The imaginary and real part of the third-order nonlinear susceptibilities were measured by a Z-scan technique. As shown in Fig. 4(a) and (c), the open- and closed- aperture curves were obtained, and the nonlinear absorption coefficient (β) and nonlinear refraction index (γ) were calculated at the wavelength from 720 nm to 920 nm. The normalized open-and closed-aperture transmittance T OP and T CL as a function of the sample position Z for a standard Gaussian laser beam is given by [41],

where q 0 = βI 0Leff, ΔΦ0 = γkI 0, and Leff = [1-exp(-αL)]/α is the effective thickness of the sample. L is the real thickness of the sample, I 0 is the peak irradiance at the focus point (z = 0), z 0 = nω 0 2/2 is Rayleigh length, ω 0 is radius of the beam spot at focus (z = 0), and k is the wave number.

As shown in Fig. 4(a), the peak-to-valley curves indicate negative γ values due to self-focusing effect. In the wavelength region of 720–920 nm, γ does not change. On the other hand, the normalized transmission curve in Fig. 4(b) shows a symmetrical valley, thus the value of β is positive, which indicate reverse saturable absorption. At longer wavelengths, the nonlinear absorption becomes smaller and almost zero at 840–920 nm. This result is due to multiphoton absorption process. In Fig. 4(d), the peak-to-valley relative transmission (ΔT = 1-T(0)) as a function of the incident laser power was given. The slope of 2.2 indicates a two-photon absorption (TPA) which dominate the nonlinear absorption process. This TPA decreases at longer wavelengths, which agrees with the tendency of linear absorption in Fig. 1.

The small linear and nonlinear absorption, as well as large nonlinear refraction indicate the GOQDs could be applied for all-optical switching with high switching efficiency and low loss feature. Two key parameters, the one-photon (W) and two-photon figures of merit (T), are calculated to evaluate the optical switching performance, where W = γI/αλ and T = βλ/γ. For an optical switching device, the conditions W > 1 and T < 1 should be satisfied. The values of W and T versus wavelength are presented in Fig. 5. In the wavelength range from 720 nm to 920 nm, W is 4.4–7.8 and T is smaller than 0.2, which extremely satisfies the requirements of optical switching device. Besides, as shown in Table 1, among the carbon-based nano-materials, our diluted GOQDs glass exhibited the largest nonlinear refraction index and the smallest nonlinear absorption coefficient in a broad operating wavelength range. The quality is comparable to that of the Ag nanocubes but with much lower cost, suggesting this material could be quite promising applied for optical switching.

We investigated the linear and nonlinear optical properties of GOQDs. Through the comprehensive excitation-emission spectrum, the two electronic transition paths from valence band to conduction band at 250 nm and 460 nm are verified, two adjacent excited states with the emission wavelength of 514 and 540 nm are discovered. These excited states are found closely related to interaction between the QDs and dispersing environment. When the GOQDs are dispersed in ormosil gel glasses, more excited bands could be observed in longer wavelength region. The photoluminescence shifts with GOQDs concentration due to self-absorption. The GOQDs also show strong nonlinear refraction and weak nonlinear absorption in the wavelength range from 720 nm to 920 nm. The diluted GOQDs glass with ideal figure of merit parameters could be used for low-cost all-optical switching devices in the near-infrared region.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 11104097) and the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant no. 2016YFA0401704).
